1: The people of the land take Jehoahaz son of Josiah and make him king in Jerusalem in his father’s place. ʿAm hāʾāreṣ (the people of the land) — the collective designation for the native Judahite population who bypass the normal succession to install Josiah’s son (Jehoahaz was younger than his brother Jehoiakim, 2 Kgs 23:31, 36). Popular installation of a king — outside the usual succession — signals the political instability that will characterize the final decades. The Chronicler’s four-king run begins with this popular but short-lived choice.
2: Jehoahaz was twenty-three years old when he began to reign, and he reigned three months in Jerusalem. The brevity — three months — is the Chronicler’s measure of YHWH’s judgment on an unworthy king. The short reigns of the last kings (Jehoahaz 3 months, Jehoiachin 3 months) bracket the slightly longer evil reigns of Jehoiakim (11 years) and Zedekiah (11 years), creating a structural symmetry: two brief bookends, two extended-evil middles.
3: The king of Egypt wayyᵃsîrēhû bîrûšālaim — deposed him at Jerusalem and imposed a tribute of 100 talents of silver and a talent of gold. Jehoahaz is removed not by Babylonian power (the coming threat) but by Egyptian — the old empire asserting control over the corridor between its territory and Mesopotamia. The tribute (wayyitten ʿal-hāʾāreṣ ʿŏneš) imposed on Judah is the economic mark of vassalage, the material consequence of covenant faithlessness.
4: Neco makes Jehoiakim, Jehoahaz’s brother, king over Judah and Jerusalem, and takes Jehoahaz to Egypt. Wayyaḥăpōḵ nᵉkô ʾet-šᵉmô — Neco changes Jehoahaz’s name (presumably to Shallum, his pre-throne name; the Chronicler mentions only the throne name). A foreign power renaming the king of Judah is the visible symbol of sovereignty transferred: the king is a vassal whose identity is shaped by his overlord.
5: Jehoiakim was twenty-five when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. Wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw — he did evil in the eyes of YHWH his God. The Chronicler’s verdict on Jehoiakim is compressed into the regnal formula without elaboration — his eleven years of evil are summarized in the same phrase used for the worst kings. What the Chronicler emphasizes is not the specific sins but the outcome: Nebuchadnezzar comes against him (v6).
6: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon comes up against Jehoiakim and binds him (wayyᵃʾasrēhû bᵉnaḥuštatayim — in bronze chains) to take him to Babylon. The bronze chains are the language of humiliation — the same instrument used on Manasseh (2 Chr 33:11) and later on Zedekiah (v13-implied; 2 Kgs 25:7). Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH’s instrument of judgment appears here for the first time in Chronicles — the Babylonian king who will dominate the remainder of the chapter.
7: Nebuchadnezzar takes some of the vessels of YHWH’s house to Babylon and puts them in his palace in Babylon. ûmikkᵉlê bêt-YHWH — from the vessels of the house of YHWH. The removal of temple vessels — the sacred objects that represent YHWH’s presence among his people — is the beginning of the desecration that culminates in the full destruction of ch36:17-19. The progressive removal of vessels (vv7, 10, 18) maps the stages of divine withdrawal from the temple.
8: The rest of Jehoiakim’s acts, the abominations he committed, and what was found against him, are written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah. Wᵉhannimṣāʾ ʿālāyw — “and what was found against him” — a unique phrase suggesting a legal dossier of offenses. The Chronicler does not elaborate but signals that the record exists and is available. His son Jehoiachin reigns in his place.
9: Jehoiachin was eighteen years old when he began to reign (the MT; the LXX and 2 Kgs 24:8 say eight), and he reigned three months and ten days in Jerusalem. Wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH — he did evil. The brevity of his reign (three months and ten days — one of the most precise short-reign records in Chronicles) signals that he is barely given time to establish himself before the Babylonian judgment falls.
10: At the turn of the year, Nebuchadnezzar sends and brings Jehoiachin to Babylon, with the precious vessels of YHWH’s house. Wayyaʿălēhû bāḇelāh ʿim kᵉlê ḥemḏat bêt-YHWH — “and brought him to Babylon with the precious things of the house of YHWH.” Kᵉlê ḥemḏat = vessels of desire/preciousness — the choicest temple objects. With each king’s fall another tranche of temple vessels leaves Jerusalem: the sacred is being drained from the holy city. Zedekiah, Jehoiachin’s brother, is made king.
11: Zedekiah was twenty-one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. Zedekiah (Ṣiḏqiyyāhû — “YHWH is my righteousness”) is given a name that his reign fails to embody. The irony of the name echoes throughout the fall narrative: the king whose name claims YHWH’s righteousness as his own is precisely the king whose rebellion against YHWH’s prophets occasions the final judgment.
12: Wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw wᵉlōʾ kānaʿ millipnê Yirmᵉyāhû hannāḇîʾ mippî YHWH — “He did evil in the sight of YHWH his God. He did not humble himself (kānaʿ) before Jeremiah the prophet, who spoke from the mouth of YHWH.” Kānaʿ (to humble/submit) is the Chronicler’s key word for the covenantal posture that averts judgment (2 Chr 7:14; 12:6, 7, 12; 30:11; 33:12, 19, 23; 34:27). Every king who kānaʿ received mercy; Zedekiah’s refusal to kānaʿ seals the exile.
13: wayyašqē ʾet-ʿarᵉpô wayyᵃʾammēṣ ʾet-libbō mišûḇ ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl — “He stiffened his neck and hardened his heart against turning (mišûḇ) to YHWH the God of Israel.” Two idioms of obstinacy: qāšāh ʿōrep (stiffened neck — the classic phrase from Exod 32:9; Deut 9:6; Jer 7:26) and ʾāmaṣ lēḇāḇ (hardened heart — an unusual form; normally ḥāzaq is used for hardening). He also rebels against Nebuchadnezzar who had made him swear by God (ʾăšer hišbîʿô bēʾlōhîm) — oath-breaking compounds covenant unfaithfulness.
14: gam kol-śārê hakḵōhănîm wᵉhāʿām hirḇû limaʿôl maʿal kᵉḵōl tôʿᵃḇôt haggôyim — “All the leading priests and the people likewise were exceedingly unfaithful (hirḇû limaʿôl maʿal), following all the abominations of the nations.” Maʿal — the Chronicler’s signature word for covenant treachery (unfaithfulness, 29x in Chronicles) — here reaches its intensified form: hirḇû limaʿôl (multiplied unfaithfulness), spread from king to priests to people. The contamination is total; the judgment must be total.
15: wᵉYHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇōtêhem šālaḥ ʾălêhem bᵉyaḏ-malʾākāyw hašḵēm wᵉšālôaḥ kî-ḥāmal ʿal-ʿammô wᵉʿal-mᵉʿônô — “The LORD, the God of their fathers, sent persistently to them by his messengers, because he had compassion (ḥāmal) on his people and on his dwelling place.” Hašḵēm wᵉšālôaḥ (lit. rising early and sending) is the idiom from Jeremiah (7:25; 25:4; 26:5; 29:19; 35:15; 44:4) for persistent prophetic outreach. The prophets are sent by divine compassion (ḥāmal — a tender, protective love) — YHWH’s sending is not mechanical judgment but the last acts of love before judgment falls.
16: wᵉhēm hayu maʿălîlîm bᵉmalʾăḵê hāʾĕlōhîm ûḇôzîm dᵉḇārāyw ûmitōlᵉlîm binᵉḇîʾāyw ʿaḏ ʿălôt ḥamat-YHWH bᵉʿammô ʿaḏ lēʾên marpēʾ — “But they kept mocking (maʿălîlîm) the messengers of God, despising (ḇôzîm) his words and scoffing (mōtîlîm) at his prophets, until the wrath of the LORD rose against his people, until there was no remedy (ʿaḏ lēʾên marpēʾ).” Three verbs of prophetic rejection (mock, despise, scoff) build to the terminal diagnosis: lēʾên marpēʾ — no healing, no remedy. The same root as rāpāʾ (to heal) negated: beyond healing. The patient has refused every treatment; the physician can only stand back.
17: Therefore God brings up against them the king of the Chaldeans — wayyaʿal ʿălêhem ʾet-melek Kaśdiyyim. Nebuchadnezzar kills their young men with the sword in the temple sanctuary, has no compassion on young man or virgin, old man or aged — hakkōl nātan bᵉyāḏô — God gives all into his hand. The destruction is comprehensive: no demographic exemption, no mercy. YHWH himself gives his people into Nebuchadnezzar’s hand. The same YHWH who compassionately sent prophets (v15) now delivers his people to the sword when the prophets are rejected.
18: All the vessels of God’s house, great and small, and the treasures of YHWH’s house, and the treasures of the king and his princes — Nebuchadnezzar brings all to Babylon. ʾet kol kᵉlê bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm hagᵉḏōlîm wᵉhaqqᵉṭannîm — great and small, without exception. The complete removal of sacred objects completes the process begun in vv7 and 10: the temple is physically stripped. The vessels that Cyrus will later restore (Ezra 1:7-11) are the link between exile and restoration — physical objects that carry the memory of YHWH’s presence through the darkness.
19: They burn the house of God, break down the wall of Jerusalem, burn all its palaces with fire, and destroy all its precious vessels. Wayyiśrᵉpû ʾet-bêt hāʾĕlōhîm wayyîṭṭᵉšû ʾet-ḥômat Yᵉrûšālaim wᵉḵol-ʾarᵉmᵉnôtêhā śārᵉpû ḇāʾēš. Four destructions: temple burned, wall broken, palaces burned, vessels destroyed. The comprehensive destruction — no structure left standing, no object left intact — is the Chronicler’s most concentrated horror: the place where YHWH’s glory dwelt, where David planned and Solomon built and Hezekiah and Josiah reformed, is ash.
20: Those who escape the sword go into exile in Babylon, where they become servants to Nebuchadnezzar and his sons until the establishment of the kingdom of Persia. Wayyiglû lāḇāḇlāh — they were exiled to Babylon. The remnant that survives the sword (sword is the first covenant curse; exile is the ultimate) serves (wayᵃhîyû-lô laʿăḇāḏîm) the Babylonian empire. The servants of YHWH become the servants of the king of Babylon — the covenant consequence for a people who refused to serve their true Lord.
21: lᵉmalōʾ dᵉḇar-YHWH bᵉpî Yirmᵉyāhû ʿaḏ-rāṣᵉtāh hāʾāreṣ ʾet-šabbᵉtōtêhā kol-yᵉmê hašammāh šāḇātāh lᵉmalōʾ šiḇʿîm šānāh — “to fulfill the word of the LORD by the mouth of Jeremiah, until the land had enjoyed its Sabbaths. All the days that it lay desolate it kept Sabbath, to fulfill seventy years.” Rāṣāh ʾet-šabbᵉtōtêhā — the land “paid off/enjoyed” its Sabbath years. The verb rāṣāh (to be pleased with, to accept, to repay a debt) is used for the satisfaction of a creditor. Lev 26:34-35 promised this enforced Sabbath; the land’s forced rest for seventy years is the fulfillment of both Lev 26 and Jer 25:11; 29:10.
22: ûḇišnat ʾaḥat lᵉKōreš melek Pāras liḵlôt dᵉḇar-YHWH mippî Yirmᵉyāhû hēʿîr YHWH ʾet-rûaḥ Kōreš melek Pāras — “Now in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that the word of the LORD by the mouth of Jeremiah might be fulfilled, the LORD stirred up (hēʿîr) the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia.” Hēʿîr ʾet-rûaḥ (stirred up the spirit) — the divine stirring of a human agent’s spirit to accomplish YHWH’s purpose (Hag 1:14; Ezra 1:1; Isa 45:13). As YHWH stirred the spirit of the Babylonian king to punish (v17), he now stirs the spirit of the Persian king to restore. The God of the exile is also the God of the return.
23: kōh-ʾāmar Kōreš melek Pāras kōl mamlᵉḵôt hāʾāreṣ nātan lî YHWH ʾĕlōhê haššāmayim wᵉhûʾ-pāqaḏ ʿālay liḇnôt-lô ḇayit bîrûšālaim... mî-ḇāḵem mikkol-ʿammô YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw ʿimmô wᵉyaʿal — “Thus says Cyrus king of Persia: The LORD, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and he has charged me to build him a house at Jerusalem, which is in Judah. Whoever is among you of all his people, may the LORD his God be with him. Let him go up (wᵉyaʿal).” The final word of the Hebrew Bible in its canonical order is wᵉyaʿal — “let him go up.” The Hebrew canon ends not with a period but with an open imperative of ascent: the invitation has been given; the response awaits; the story is not yet finished. The canon closes mid-sentence in divine invitation.
